text: During diagnostic laparoscopy, dense peritumoral inflammatory adhesions are observed around a bulky sigmoid colon cancer. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook diatomy to carefully remove these adhesions and mobilize the tumor laterally, taking an additional cuff of peritoneum for adequate lateral margins. The left ureter is identified, displaced laterally by the tumor. The surgeon dissects layer by layer to maintain the correct plane, distorted by adhesions and tumor weight. A loop of small bowel adhered to the mass is gently dissected free using scissors.

During diagnostic laparoscopy, dense peritumoral inflammatory adhesions are observed around a bulky sigmoid colon cancer. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook diathermy to carefully remove these adhesions and mobilize the tumor laterally, ensuring adequate lateral margins by taking an additional cuff of peritoneum. The left ureter, displaced laterally by the tumor, is identified. The surgeon dissects the plane layer by layer to maintain the correct surgical plane, which is distorted by adhesions and tumor weight. A loop of small bowel adhered to the mass is gently dissected free using scissors.

The surgeon turns the patient to the left, using gravity and gentle retraction to facilitate medial dissection. Peritumoral inflammatory adhesions are removed, revealing the tumor densely adhered to the small bowel mesentery. The small bowel mesentery peritoneum is dissected en bloc with the tumor for adequate resection margins. The splenic flexure is mobilized by reflecting the greater omentum superiorly and dissecting between the greater omentum and transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. The splenic flexure is freed by resecting the spinal colic ligament.

The surgeon completes lateral mobilization and begins medial dissection by turning the patient to the left, using gravity and gentle retraction to remove peritumoral inflammatory adhesions. The tumor, densely adhered and possibly invading the small bowel mesentery, is dissected en bloc with the mesentery to ensure adequate resection margins. The splenic flexure is mobilized by reflecting the greater omentum superiorly and dissecting between the greater omentum and transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. The splenic flexure is freed by resecting the spinal colic ligament.

The surgeon dissects the tumor from its posterior retroperitoneal attachments, revealing the intact fascia overlying the tail of the pancreas and the left kidney. The posterior and lateral mobilization of the tumor is completed, and the posterior medial dissection continues.

The surgeon dissects the tumor from its posterior retroperitoneal attachments, exposing the intact fascia over the tail of the pancreas and the left kidney. The posterior and lateral mobilization of the tumor is completed, and the posterior media dissection is continued.

The surgeon introduces a hand port through an extension of the left alec to improve retraction and facilitate the final dissection of the bulky tumor. The hand is used to enhance retraction, ensuring safe completion of the dissection without obstructing the surgical field.

The surgeon introduces a hand port through an extension of the left alec to improve retraction and facilitate the final dissection of the bulky tumor. The hand is used to safely complete the dissection after ensuring the tumor is adequately mobilized in all directions.
The surgeon uses palpation to safely dissect the tumor free from its remaining posterior attachments, utilizing a laparoscopic-assisted approach for precise mobilization and enhanced visualization.

The surgeon uses palpation to safely dissect the tumor free from its remaining posterior attachments, utilizing a laparoscopic-assisted approach for precise mobilization and enhanced visualization of the bulky colon cancer.

The surgeon locates the inferior mesenteric artery and vein for high ligation using a vessel sealing device, with the mesocolon divided to achieve proximal margins. The sigmoid colon cancer is retracted cranially, and distal total mesorectal excision is performed using sharp dissection until adequate distal margins are achieved. The specimen is extracted through the hand port sleeve protector in the left iliac fossa. Final abdominal incisions are visible, with a local anesthetic infusion catheter placed into the main abdominal wound.
